restore-without-rewriting-history.label: Recover from Everyday Mistakes
restore-without-rewriting-history.summary: Restore one unstaged mistake and one staged mistake without rewriting history.

sketch2.title: A Staged Mistake May Need Two Actions

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Restore an Unstaged README Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
Open `community-project/README.md` and find:

`Shared community activities and updates.`

Replace it with:

`Accidental rewrite.`

Save the file. Before undoing anything, inspect the unstaged change in `README.md` so you can see exactly what would be discarded.

Then restore the file:

```bash
git restore README.md
```

By default, `git restore` replaces the working copy with the version currently in the staging area. In this exercise, that staged version matches the current commit, so the accidental rewrite is removed and the committed README returns.

This command is useful when an unstaged edit is unwanted, but use it carefully: it discards the local change rather than saving it for later.

Finally, inspect the short status and confirm that no tracked change remains.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Review the mistake first, then restore only the working-tree copy.

help.concept: `git restore README.md` replaces the unstaged working copy with the version from the current commit. It does not create, delete, or rewrite earlier commits.
help.hint_1: Use `git diff -- README.md` before restoring so you can see what will be discarded.
help.hint_2: Ignored generated files may remain on disk without appearing in normal status.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.solutionCode:
git diff -- README.md
git restore README.md
git status --short
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions: Open `community-project/README.md`. Find `Shared community activities and updates.` and replace it with `Accidental rewrite.` Save the file. Inspect its unstaged diff, restore the committed README, and confirm with short status that no tracked change remains.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Add community contacts'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community schedule'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0130
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-04T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-04T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community contacts'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-05T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-05T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Organize community project files'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-06T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-06T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Add generated summary'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'generated/
' > community-project/.gitignore
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-07T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-07T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Ignore generated output'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'generated/
' > community-project/.gitignore
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt
git -C community-project add -A
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'generated/
' > community-project/.gitignore
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you inspect the README mistake before discarding it?

1.message: Did you restore only the README working copy?

2.message: Did you verify that no tracked change remains?

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Stage and Unstage an Events Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
Open `community-project/events.txt` and find:

`Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM`

Replace it with:

`Saturday cleanup: midnight`

Save the file, then stage the change:

```bash
git add events.txt
```

At this point, the mistaken edit is part of the proposed next snapshot.

Remove it from the staging area by running:

```bash
git restore --staged events.txt
```

The `--staged` option changes only the staging layer. The edit remains in the working copy of `events.txt`, but it is no longer prepared for the next commit.

Do not restore the file’s text yet.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Stage the supplied mistake, then correct only the staging layer.

help.concept: `git restore --staged events.txt` replaces the staged version with the version from `HEAD` while preserving the current working-tree text. The unwanted edit therefore remains visible but unstaged.
help.hint_1: Do not run plain `git restore events.txt` in this step.
help.hint_2: The next exercise will restore the working copy.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.solutionCode:
git add events.txt
git restore --staged events.txt
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions: Open `community-project/events.txt`. Find `Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM` and replace it with `Saturday cleanup: midnight`. Save the file. Stage the mistake, then remove it from the staging area without changing the working file. Do not restore the text yet.

find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'generated/
' > community-project/.gitignore

' > community-project/README.md

Did you stage the supplied events mistake?

1.message: Did you unstage it without changing the working file?

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Restore the Remaining Events Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
Now discard that unwanted edit and restore the file to the version stored in the current commit:

```bash
git restore events.txt
```

This second restore changes the working file itself. Together, the two commands correct the mistake in separate steps:

* `git restore --staged events.txt` removed the change from the proposed snapshot.
* `git restore events.txt` discarded the remaining working-tree edit.

Finally, inspect the short status and confirm that no tracked changes remain unfinished.

help.concept: `git restore events.txt` replaces the remaining unstaged edit with the committed version. Because the staging area is already clean, one restore command completes the recovery.
help.hint_1: The restored line should return to `Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM`.
help.hint_2: Normal short status should be empty.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions: The incorrect midnight time remains unstaged in `events.txt`. Restore the committed file, then confirm that short status has no unfinished tracked changes.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you restore the remaining working-tree mistake?

1.message: Did you verify the clean tracked state?

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git restore
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of `git restore path` in this course?
single-choice-1.hint: Without `--staged`, `git restore` replaces an unwanted working-tree edit with the recorded version.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To discard an unwanted working-tree edit

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git restore --staged
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use `git restore --staged` on a file?
single-choice-2.hint: The `--staged` option changes the staging area while preserving the current working-tree text.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The file is removed from the staging area but changes are kept
options.b: The file is deleted from the repository
options.c: The file is committed to the repository
options.d: The file is reverted to the last committed state

multi-choice-1.title: Choosing the Correct Restore Command
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can discard an unwanted working-tree edit and unstage a file?
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Discard a Change That Is Already Staged
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the safe correction steps for a file whose accidental rewrite is both staged and present in the working tree.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Remove the accidental version from the staging area
tokens.t2: Restore the working file from the recorded version

restore-sketch-0.title: Restore an Unstaged File
restore-sketch-0.bodyMarkdown: Sometimes you change a tracked file and then decide that you do not want to keep the edit. Git can restore the file to its previously recorded state without creating another commit.

restore-sketch-1.title: Unstage Without Losing the Edit
restore-sketch-1.bodyMarkdown: Sometimes a change is staged before you realize it should not be included in the next commit. Git lets you remove that change from the staging area without discarding the work from the file itself.

restore-sketch-2.title: Then Restore the Working Copy
restore-sketch-2.bodyMarkdown: The incorrect midnight time is no longer staged, but it still remains in the working copy of `events.txt`.
